dispatch.return_message: Across all three rounds, the agent was trapped in a representation ceiling — every attempt used hand-crafted per-residue physicochemical lookup tables that encode only amino acid identity, not the 3D structural context (burial depth, hydrogen-bond networks, backbone strain) that actually drives deltaTm, so no model family (MLP, GBR, RF) or feature enrichment within that tabular paradigm could move MAE below ~64°C on a target whose typical range is ±20°C.

prompt.content:
You are a research-failure curator producing a **deep negative-knowledge
record** that summarises a 3-round Self-Debug exploration of one
ScienceAgentBench task. All three rounds failed.

The 3-round exploration was a strong baseline: at round 2 the agent saw
its round-1 code + full stderr + eval output (covering memory); at round
3 it saw the round-2 code + full stderr + eval output. Despite this, all
three attempts failed. Your NK record should reflect that this is *not*
a trivial one-shot failure — three different concrete approaches have
been ruled out.

# Task

Task id: `078`

Task instruction:
> Train a neural network model using the pucci dataset to predict the melting temperature (deltaTm) of proteins caused due to the point mutation of one amino acid at chain A. The dataset also contains the PDB files which need to be processed to retrieve the original (wild-type) amino acid sequence and then to create the mutated sequences. Save the predictions for the test set as a single column dataframe "deltaTm" to the file "pred_results/pucci-proteins_test_pred.csv".

# Inputs you may read (and only these)

You will read up to 11 files. Do not read anything else.

**Round 1 (no memory, free attempt):**
1. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_078/primary_4.6/v3/round1/candidate.py` — round-1 script
2. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_078/primary_4.6/v3/round1/exec.log` — stdout / stderr / runtime errors
3. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_078/primary_4.6/v3/round1/eval.log` — evaluator output IF it ran
4. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_078/primary_4.6/v3/round1/reasoning.md` — round-1 agent's reasoning

**Round 2 (Self-Debug covering memory of round 1):**
5. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_078/primary_4.6/v3/round2_B2/candidate.py`
6. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_078/primary_4.6/v3/round2_B2/exec.log`
7. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_078/primary_4.6/v3/round2_B2/eval.log` — eval.log status for round 2: Present — read it.
8. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_078/primary_4.6/v3/round2_B2/reasoning.md`

**Round 3 (Self-Debug covering memory of round 2):**
9. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_078/primary_4.6/v3/round3_B2/candidate.py`
10. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_078/primary_4.6/v3/round3_B2/exec.log`
11. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_078/primary_4.6/v3/round3_B2/eval.log` — eval.log status for round 3: Present — read it.
12. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_078/primary_4.6/v3/round3_B2/reasoning.md`

Read all available files before writing.

# Your output (exactly one Write call)

Write a JSON file via the Write tool to:

`/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/Negative_Knowledge/section3/04_outputs/nk_records/task_078_deep.json`

The schema extends the base NK with two fields specific to deep curation:

```json
{
  "task_id": "078",
  "depth": 3,
  "rounds_summary": [
    {
      "round": 1,
      "attempted_route": "<= 200 chars; what method/library/parameter was tried",
      "observation":     "<= 200 chars; specific failure signature"
    },
    {"round": 2, "attempted_route": "...", "observation": "..."},
    {"round": 3, "attempted_route": "...", "observation": "..."}
  ],
  "ruled_out_routes": [
    "<= 150 chars per item; specific (library + parameter) combinations that have been empirically shown not to work on this task. List 2-4 items.",
    "..."
  ],
  "synthesised_diagnosis": "<= 400 chars; ONE coherent mechanism-level explanation that accounts for ALL THREE failures. Not a per-round list; a unified diagnosis. This is the load-bearing claim of the deep NK — make it specific.",
  "failure": {
    "layer": "implementation_failure | communication_failure | method_failure",
    "scope": "local_failure | regime_bound_failure | general_failure",
    "degree": "contradicted | partial | inconclusive | unstable | artifact_driven | overclaimed",
    "recommended_action": "retry | change_method | narrow_claim | abandon_route",
    "risk": "low_risk_omission | medium_risk_drift | high_risk_false_progress"
  },
  "rationale": "<= 300 chars; one mechanism-level claim explaining WHY the synthesised diagnosis is correct.",
  "recommended_alternative": "<= 400 chars; ONE specific concrete fix the next attempt should try. Must NOT be a method already in ruled_out_routes. Must name specific APIs/library functions/parameters."
}
```

# Hard rules

1. Use Read on any subset of the listed input files. Do NOT read other files.
2. Use Write tool EXACTLY ONCE, to `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/Negative_Knowledge/section3/04_outputs/nk_records/task_078_deep.json`, with the JSON object.
3. Do NOT use Bash, Edit, Grep, Glob, or any other tool.
4. Do NOT wrap in markdown code fences. The file must be parseable by `json.load`.
5. The `recommended_alternative` must NOT recommend anything that already
   appears in `ruled_out_routes`. The whole point of deep NK is that the
   next attempt avoids what's already known to fail.
6. The `failure.*` taxonomy values are fixed; do not invent new values.
7. After the single Write, respond with ONE short sentence describing the
   synthesised diagnosis that ties all three round failures together.

# Guidance

The `synthesised_diagnosis` field is the highest-value piece of a deep NK.
It is the difference between "three independent failure snapshots" and
"one coherent understanding of why this task is hard". Write it as if you
were explaining to a colleague: *"Across all three rounds, the agent kept
running into <mechanism>; it can't be fixed by <tactical patches the
agents tried> because <reason>."* The `recommended_alternative` should
then follow from this diagnosis, not from any single round's local error.

If the three rounds revealed unrelated bugs (i.e., each round fixed the
prior round's bug but exposed a new one), say so — that's also a
legitimate synthesised diagnosis ("the task has at least three
independent gotchas, of which we've now uncovered all three"), and the
`recommended_alternative` should bundle fixes for all three.

r1_candidate.content:
"""
Predict deltaTm for protein point mutations using the pucci dataset.
Pipeline: parse PDB chain-A sequences -> build mutant sequences -> featurize -> train MLP -> predict test set.
"""
import os
import re
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.neural_network import MLPRegressor

# ── constants ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA_DIR = "benchmark/datasets/pucci"
PDB_DIR  = os.path.join(DATA_DIR, "PDBs")
OUT_DIR  = "pred_results"
OUT_FILE = os.path.join(OUT_DIR, "pucci-proteins_test_pred.csv")

# Three-letter -> one-letter amino acid mapping
THREE_TO_ONE = {
    "ALA": "A", "ARG": "R", "ASN": "N", "ASP": "D", "CYS": "C",
    "GLN": "Q", "GLU": "E", "GLY": "G", "HIS": "H", "ILE": "I",
    "LEU": "L", "LYS": "K", "MET": "M", "PHE": "F", "PRO": "P",
    "SER": "S", "THR": "T", "TRP": "W", "TYR": "Y", "VAL": "V",
    "MSE": "M",  # selenomethionine -> methionine
}

# 20 standard amino acids (alphabetical)
AA_ORDER = list("ACDEFGHIKLMNPQRSTVWY")
AA_INDEX = {aa: i for i, aa in enumerate(AA_ORDER)}

# ── helpers ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def parse_chain_a_sequence(pdb_id: str):
    """Return dict {res_number: one_letter} for chain A from the PDB file."""
    path = os.path.join(PDB_DIR, f"{pdb_id.lower()}.pdb")
    seq = {}
    seen = set()
    try:
        with open(path) as fh:
            for line in fh:
                if not line.startswith("ATOM"):
                    continue
                chain = line[21]
                if chain != "A":
                    continue
                res3 = line[17:20].strip()
                try:
                    resn = int(line[22:26].strip())
                except ValueError:
                    continue
                aa1 = THREE_TO_ONE.get(res3)
                if aa1 and resn not in seen:
                    seen.add(resn)
                    seq[resn] = aa1
    except FileNotFoundError:
        pass
    return seq


def make_mutant_sequence(wt_seq: dict, resn: int, mut_aa1: str):
    """Apply a point mutation to the wild-type sequence dict and return list of aa."""
    seq = dict(wt_seq)
    if resn in seq:
        seq[resn] = mut_aa1
    return [seq[k] for k in sorted(seq.keys())]


def composition_features(seq_list):
    """20-dim amino acid composition vector."""
    arr = np.zeros(20)
    if not seq_list:
        return arr
    for aa in seq_list:
        if aa in AA_INDEX:
            arr[AA_INDEX[aa]] += 1
    arr /= len(seq_list)
    return arr


def build_features(df: pd.DataFrame, wt_seqs: dict):
    """
    Build feature matrix for every row in df.
    Features (per row):
      - 20 composition features of mutant sequence
      - Tmexp [wt]  (wild-type melting temperature)
      - sequence length
      - wild-type residue one-hot (20 dims)
      - mutant residue one-hot (20 dims)
    Total: 63 features
    """
    rows = []
    for _, row in df.iterrows():
        pdb_id  = str(row["PDBid"]).lower()
        resn    = int(row["RESN"])
        res_wt  = str(row["RESwt"]).strip()
        res_mut = str(row["RESmut"]).strip()
        tm_wt   = float(row["Tmexp [wt]"]) if not pd.isna(row["Tmexp [wt]"]) else 0.0

        wt_aa1  = THREE_TO_ONE.get(res_wt, "G")
        mut_aa1 = THREE_TO_ONE.get(res_mut, "G")

        wt_seq = wt_seqs.get(pdb_id, {})
        mut_seq_list = make_mutant_sequence(wt_seq, resn, mut_aa1)

        comp = composition_features(mut_seq_list)
        seq_len = len(mut_seq_list) if mut_seq_list else 100

        wt_oh  = np.zeros(20)
        mut_oh = np.zeros(20)
        if wt_aa1  in AA_INDEX: wt_oh [AA_INDEX[wt_aa1 ]] = 1.0
        if mut_aa1 in AA_INDEX: mut_oh[AA_INDEX[mut_aa1]] = 1.0

        feat = np.concatenate([comp, [tm_wt, seq_len], wt_oh, mut_oh])
        rows.append(feat)
    return np.array(rows, dtype=np.float32)


# ── main ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def main():
    os.makedirs(OUT_DIR, exist_ok=True)

    # Load CSVs
    train_df = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA_DIR, "pucci-proteins_train.csv"), index_col=0)
    test_df  = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA_DIR, "pucci-proteins_test.csv"),  index_col=0)
    dev_df   = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA_DIR, "pucci-proteins_dev.csv"),   index_col=0)

    # Combine train + dev for fitting
    fit_df = pd.concat([train_df, dev_df], ignore_index=True)

    # Parse all needed PDB files once
    all_pdb_ids = set(fit_df["PDBid"].str.lower().unique()) | set(test_df["PDBid"].str.lower().unique())
    wt_seqs = {pid: parse_chain_a_sequence(pid) for pid in all_pdb_ids}

    # Build feature matrices
    X_train = build_features(fit_df,  wt_seqs)
    X_test  = build_features(test_df, wt_seqs)

    # Target: ΔTmexp column (may be named with unicode delta)
    target_col = None
    for col in fit_df.columns:
        if "Tm" in col and col.startswith("Δ"):
            target_col = col
            break
    if target_col is None:
        # Fallback: find column whose name starts with 'delta' or is exactly 'ΔTmexp'
        for col in fit_df.columns:
            low = col.lower()
            if "deltaTm" in col or ("Δ" in col and "exp" in col):
                target_col = col
                break
    if target_col is None:
        # Last resort: use 4th column after index
        target_col = fit_df.columns[3]

    y_train = fit_df[target_col].astype(float).values

    # Standardise features
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    X_train_s = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
    X_test_s  = scaler.transform(X_test)

    # MLP regressor
    model = MLPRegressor(
        hidden_layer_sizes=(256, 128, 64),
        activation="relu",
        solver="adam",
        max_iter=500,
        learning_rate_init=1e-3,
        early_stopping=True,
        validation_fraction=0.1,
        random_state=42,
        n_iter_no_change=20,
    )
    model.fit(X_train_s, y_train)

    # Predict and save
    preds = model.predict(X_test_s)
    out_df = pd.DataFrame({"deltaTm": preds})
    out_df.to_csv(OUT_FILE, index=False)
    print(f"Saved {len(preds)} predictions to {OUT_FILE}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

r1_reasoning.content:
# Reasoning

## Approach

The task requires predicting the change in melting temperature (deltaTm) caused by a single point mutation at chain A of a protein.

1. **PDB parsing**: For each unique PDB id, parse all `ATOM` records on chain A to reconstruct the wild-type residue sequence as a dictionary `{residue_number: one_letter_aa}`. Only the first occurrence of each residue number is kept (avoids duplicate atoms).
2. **Mutant sequence construction**: For each row in the dataset, look up the wild-type sequence, replace the residue at the given position with the mutant amino acid, and produce an ordered list of one-letter amino acids.
3. **Featurisation**: Each mutant sequence is represented by:
   - 20-dimensional amino acid composition vector (fraction of each standard amino acid in the mutant sequence)
   - Wild-type melting temperature `Tmexp [wt]` (strong prior for deltaTm magnitude)
   - Sequence length
   - One-hot encoding of the wild-type amino acid (20 dims)
   - One-hot encoding of the mutant amino acid (20 dims)
   Total: 62 features.
4. **Model**: A three-layer MLP (`MLPRegressor` from scikit-learn) with hidden sizes (256, 128, 64), ReLU activations, Adam optimiser, and early stopping. Train and dev sets are combined for fitting.
5. **Output**: Predictions for the test set are written as a single-column CSV `pred_results/pucci-proteins_test_pred.csv` with header `deltaTm`.

## Library choices

- **pandas / numpy**: data loading and numerical operations — universally available and sufficient.
- **scikit-learn `MLPRegressor`**: a multi-layer perceptron with regularisation via early stopping; no GPU dependency, deterministic with a fixed seed, and fits quickly on a dataset of this size.
- **Standard library `re`, `os`**: PDB parsing and path handling.

PyTorch/Keras could yield a marginally better model but would add complexity without clear benefit at this data scale (~1000–2000 training rows). `deepchem` or `chemprop` are targeted at small molecules rather than protein sequences, so they were not used.

## Risks

- **PDB residue numbering gaps**: residue numbers in PDB files are not always contiguous; the mutation position `RESN` may not exactly match the parsed key. If the key is absent, the mutation is silently skipped (wild-type composition is used), which slightly degrades accuracy for those rows.
- **Non-standard residues**: only residues in `THREE_TO_ONE` are included; others are dropped. This may shorten the effective sequence.
- **Feature expressiveness**: amino acid composition discards positional information. A more powerful approach (e.g. ESM embeddings) would require large pretrained models not in the available package list.
- **Target column name**: the target uses a Unicode delta character (ΔTmexp); the code tries multiple fallback strategies to locate it.

## Use of memory

No prior memory was available for this task (first attempt). All design choices were made from scratch based on the task description and domain knowledge provided in the prompt.

r2_candidate.content:
"""
Predict deltaTm for protein point mutations using the pucci dataset.
Round 2 — improved features and model after round-1 MAE of 67.

Key fixes vs round 1:
  - Robust target-column detection (handles various Unicode/encoding variants of the Δ symbol).
  - Rich physicochemical per-residue features (hydrophobicity, charge, MW, volume, polarity).
  - Differential features: change in property between WT and mutant residue.
  - Tmexp [wt] is kept as a primary numeric feature.
  - Train + dev combined; StandardScaler + MLPRegressor with more iterations.
"""

import os
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.neural_network import MLPRegressor
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# ── paths ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA_DIR = "benchmark/datasets/pucci"
PDB_DIR  = os.path.join(DATA_DIR, "PDBs")
OUT_DIR  = "pred_results"
OUT_FILE = os.path.join(OUT_DIR, "pucci-proteins_test_pred.csv")

# ── amino acid dictionaries ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THREE_TO_ONE = {
    "ALA": "A", "ARG": "R", "ASN": "N", "ASP": "D", "CYS": "C",
    "GLN": "Q", "GLU": "E", "GLY": "G", "HIS": "H", "ILE": "I",
    "LEU": "L", "LYS": "K", "MET": "M", "PHE": "F", "PRO": "P",
    "SER": "S", "THR": "T", "TRP": "W", "TYR": "Y", "VAL": "V",
    "MSE": "M",
}

AA_ORDER = list("ACDEFGHIKLMNPQRSTVWY")
AA_INDEX = {aa: i for i, aa in enumerate(AA_ORDER)}

# Kyte-Doolittle hydrophobicity (scale: -4.5 to 4.5)
HYDROPHOBICITY = {
    "A":  1.8, "R": -4.5, "N": -3.5, "D": -3.5, "C":  2.5,
    "Q": -3.5, "E": -3.5, "G": -0.4, "H": -3.2, "I":  4.5,
    "L":  3.8, "K": -3.9, "M":  1.9, "F":  2.8, "P": -1.6,
    "S": -0.8, "T": -0.7, "W": -0.9, "Y": -1.3, "V":  4.2,
}

# Formal charge at pH 7
CHARGE = {
    "A":  0, "R": +1, "N":  0, "D": -1, "C":  0,
    "Q":  0, "E": -1, "G":  0, "H":  0, "I":  0,
    "L":  0, "K": +1, "M":  0, "F":  0, "P":  0,
    "S":  0, "T":  0, "W":  0, "Y":  0, "V":  0,
}

# Molecular weight (approximate, Da)
MOL_WEIGHT = {
    "A":  89, "R": 174, "N": 132, "D": 133, "C": 121,
    "Q": 146, "E": 147, "G":  75, "H": 155, "I": 131,
    "L": 131, "K": 146, "M": 149, "F": 165, "P": 115,
    "S": 105, "T": 119, "W": 204, "Y": 181, "V": 117,
}

# van der Waals volume (Å³, approximate)
VDW_VOLUME = {
    "A":  67, "R": 148, "N": 114, "D": 111, "C":  86,
    "Q": 128, "E": 138, "G":  48, "H": 118, "I": 124,
    "L": 124, "K": 135, "M": 124, "F": 135, "P":  90,
    "S":  73, "T":  93, "W": 163, "Y": 141, "V": 105,
}

# Polarity (Zimmermann scale, 0-52)
POLARITY = {
    "A":  0.0, "R": 52.0, "N": 3.38, "D": 49.7, "C":  1.48,
    "Q":  3.53, "E": 49.9, "G":  0.0, "H": 51.6, "I":  0.13,
    "L":  0.13, "K": 49.5, "M":  1.43, "F":  0.35, "P":  1.58,
    "S":  1.67, "T":  1.66, "W":  2.10, "Y":  1.61, "V":  0.13,
}


def aa_props(aa: str) -> np.ndarray:
    """Return 5-dim physicochemical feature vector for a single amino acid."""
    return np.array([
        HYDROPHOBICITY.get(aa, 0.0),
        CHARGE.get(aa, 0.0),
        MOL_WEIGHT.get(aa, 120.0),
        VDW_VOLUME.get(aa, 100.0),
        POLARITY.get(aa, 0.0),
    ], dtype=np.float32)


def parse_chain_a_sequence(pdb_id: str) -> dict:
    """Return {res_number: one_letter} for chain A."""
    path = os.path.join(PDB_DIR, f"{pdb_id.lower()}.pdb")
    seq = {}
    seen = set()
    try:
        with open(path) as fh:
            for line in fh:
                if not line.startswith("ATOM"):
                    continue
                if line[21] != "A":
                    continue
                res3 = line[17:20].strip()
                try:
                    resn = int(line[22:26].strip())
                except ValueError:
                    continue
                aa1 = THREE_TO_ONE.get(res3)
                if aa1 and resn not in seen:
                    seen.add(resn)
                    seq[resn] = aa1
    except FileNotFoundError:
        pass
    return seq


def make_mutant_sequence(wt_seq: dict, resn: int, mut_aa1: str) -> list:
    seq = dict(wt_seq)
    if resn in seq:
        seq[resn] = mut_aa1
    return [seq[k] for k in sorted(seq.keys())]


def composition_features(seq_list: list) -> np.ndarray:
    """20-dim amino acid composition."""
    arr = np.zeros(20, dtype=np.float32)
    if not seq_list:
        return arr
    for aa in seq_list:
        if aa in AA_INDEX:
            arr[AA_INDEX[aa]] += 1
    arr /= len(seq_list)
    return arr


def build_features(df: pd.DataFrame, wt_seqs: dict) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Feature vector per row (total = 68 dims):
      - 20: mutant sequence amino acid composition
      - 5:  wild-type residue physicochemical properties
      - 5:  mutant residue physicochemical properties
      - 5:  difference (mut - wt) in physicochemical properties
      - 20: wild-type residue one-hot (20 standard AAs)
      - 20: mutant residue one-hot
      -  1: Tmexp [wt]
      -  1: sequence length
      -  1: relative position of mutation in sequence (resn / seq_len)
    Total: 78 dims
    """
    rows = []
    for _, row in df.iterrows():
        pdb_id  = str(row["PDBid"]).lower()
        resn    = int(row["RESN"])
        res_wt  = str(row["RESwt"]).strip()
        res_mut = str(row["RESmut"]).strip()

        # Safely parse Tm_wt
        try:
            tm_wt = float(row["Tmexp [wt]"])
        except (ValueError, KeyError):
            tm_wt = 50.0  # fallback to typical value

        wt_aa1  = THREE_TO_ONE.get(res_wt,  "G")
        mut_aa1 = THREE_TO_ONE.get(res_mut, "G")

        wt_seq       = wt_seqs.get(pdb_id, {})
        mut_seq_list = make_mutant_sequence(wt_seq, resn, mut_aa1)

        comp    = composition_features(mut_seq_list)
        seq_len = len(mut_seq_list) if mut_seq_list else 100
        rel_pos = resn / seq_len if seq_len > 0 else 0.5

        props_wt  = aa_props(wt_aa1)
        props_mut = aa_props(mut_aa1)
        props_diff = props_mut - props_wt

        wt_oh  = np.zeros(20, dtype=np.float32)
        mut_oh = np.zeros(20, dtype=np.float32)
        if wt_aa1  in AA_INDEX: wt_oh [AA_INDEX[wt_aa1 ]] = 1.0
        if mut_aa1 in AA_INDEX: mut_oh[AA_INDEX[mut_aa1]] = 1.0

        scalar_feats = np.array([tm_wt, seq_len, rel_pos], dtype=np.float32)

        feat = np.concatenate([comp, props_wt, props_mut, props_diff, wt_oh, mut_oh, scalar_feats])
        rows.append(feat)

    return np.array(rows, dtype=np.float32)


def find_target_column(df: pd.DataFrame) -> str:
    """
    Robustly locate the deltaTm column in the dataframe.
    The column header in the CSV is written as 'ΔTmexp' but encoding issues
    can cause it to appear under different byte sequences.
    Priority order:
      1. Exact match 'ΔTmexp'
      2. Column whose name contains both 'Tm' and starts with any delta-like char
      3. Column index 7 (0-indexed after dropping the index column), i.e. columns[7]
         based on the CSV preview: Unnamed:0, N, PDBid, Chain, RESN, RESwt, RESmut, ΔTmexp
    """
    # Exact unicode match
    if "ΔTmexp" in df.columns:
        return "ΔTmexp"

    # Any column whose lower-case name contains 'tm' and 'exp' but not 'wt'
    for col in df.columns:
        cl = col.lower()
        if "tm" in cl and "exp" in cl and "wt" not in cl:
            return col

    # Fall back to positional: the 8th column in the original CSV
    # After read_csv(index_col=0): columns[0]=N, [1]=PDBid, [2]=Chain, [3]=RESN,
    # [4]=RESwt, [5]=RESmut, [6]=ΔTmexp  (index 6, 0-based)
    if len(df.columns) > 6:
        return df.columns[6]

    raise ValueError(f"Cannot find deltaTm column. Columns: {list(df.columns)}")


def main():
    os.makedirs(OUT_DIR, exist_ok=True)

    # Load data
    train_df = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA_DIR, "pucci-proteins_train.csv"), index_col=0)
    test_df  = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA_DIR, "pucci-proteins_test.csv"),  index_col=0)
    dev_df   = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA_DIR, "pucci-proteins_dev.csv"),   index_col=0)

    # Combine train + dev for fitting
    fit_df = pd.concat([train_df, dev_df], ignore_index=True)

    # Identify target column
    target_col = find_target_column(fit_df)
    print(f"Target column: {repr(target_col)}")

    # Parse PDB sequences once
    all_pdb_ids = (
        set(fit_df["PDBid"].str.lower().unique()) |
        set(test_df["PDBid"].str.lower().unique())
    )
    wt_seqs = {pid: parse_chain_a_sequence(pid) for pid in all_pdb_ids}

    # Build features
    X_train = build_features(fit_df,  wt_seqs)
    X_test  = build_features(test_df, wt_seqs)

    # Target
    y_train = fit_df[target_col].astype(float).values

    # Scale
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    X_train_s = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
    X_test_s  = scaler.transform(X_test)

    # MLP — deeper, more iterations, smaller LR
    model = MLPRegressor(
        hidden_layer_sizes=(512, 256, 128, 64),
        activation="relu",
        solver="adam",
        max_iter=1000,
        learning_rate_init=5e-4,
        early_stopping=True,
        validation_fraction=0.1,
        n_iter_no_change=30,
        random_state=42,
        batch_size=64,
    )
    model.fit(X_train_s, y_train)

    # Predict and save
    preds = model.predict(X_test_s)
    out_df = pd.DataFrame({"deltaTm": preds})
    out_df.to_csv(OUT_FILE, index=False)
    print(f"Saved {len(preds)} predictions to {OUT_FILE}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

r2_reasoning.content:
# Reasoning

## Approach

Round 1 succeeded technically (exit_code=0, 147 rows saved) but produced an unacceptably high MAE of 67.15. The target deltaTm values are typically in the range of about -20 to +20 °C, so an MAE of 67 indicates the model was either regressing to the wrong target column, or the feature representation was so poor that the MLP converged to a near-constant prediction far from the true mean.

The fix strategy is two-pronged: (1) make target-column detection more robust and explicit with diagnostic printing, and (2) substantially enrich the feature representation to give the model real physicochemical signal about why a point mutation shifts Tm.

Key new features added:
- **5 physicochemical properties for the wild-type residue**: Kyte-Doolittle hydrophobicity, formal charge at pH 7, molecular weight, van der Waals volume, and polarity (Zimmermann scale).
- **5 physicochemical properties for the mutant residue**: same dimensions.
- **5 differential features** (mut - wt): encode the magnitude and direction of the change induced by the mutation. These are the most directly predictive features for deltaTm.
- **Relative position** (resn / seq_len): captures whether the mutation is near the termini (often less destabilizing) or in the core.
- The wild-type Tmexp column is kept as a strong numeric anchor.
- 20-dim amino acid composition of the mutant sequence is retained.
- One-hot encodings of both WT and mutant residue identities are retained.

The total feature vector grows from 63 to 78 dimensions.

## Library choices

- `pandas` / `numpy`: standard CSV and array handling.
- `sklearn.neural_network.MLPRegressor`: available, lightweight, sufficient for tabular data with ~78 features and ~1 500 training samples. Deeper architecture (512-256-128-64) with 1000 max iterations and early stopping (patience 30) gives the model enough capacity without overfitting.
- `sklearn.preprocessing.StandardScaler`: essential for MLP convergence given the heterogeneous feature scales (composition fractions 0-1, MW in hundreds, Tm in tens).

No external bioinformatics libraries are needed because PDB parsing is straightforward ATOM-record text parsing, and all amino acid property tables are hard-coded as dictionaries.

## Risks

- **Target column ambiguity**: the CSV header "ΔTmexp" uses a Unicode delta character that can be mangled by different encodings. The `find_target_column` function tries exact match first, then a heuristic containing "tm" and "exp" but not "wt", then falls back to positional index 6 (based on the documented CSV column order). A diagnostic `print` statement will confirm which column was selected at runtime.
- **Missing PDB files**: handled with a silent `FileNotFoundError` pass; the empty-dict fallback produces a zero composition vector and a fallback seq_len of 100, which may introduce some noise for rows with missing PDB data.
- **Small dataset**: with ~1 500 training rows and 78 features, overfitting is possible. Early stopping with 10% validation fraction mitigates this.

## Use of memory

Round 1's `exec.log` showed exit_code=0 and correct output path. Round 1's `eval.log` revealed MAE of 67.15, confirming the script ran but predicted poorly. The diagnosis: either the target column lookup was off, or the feature set was too weak. This round adds richer physicochemical features and a more explicit and defensively coded target-column finder to address both failure modes.

r3_candidate.content:
"""
Predict deltaTm for protein point mutations using the pucci dataset.
Round 3 — gradient-boosted ensemble replacing MLP.

Key changes vs round 2 (MAE 64.3):
  - Replaced MLPRegressor with a stacked ensemble: GradientBoostingRegressor
    as the primary model, backed by a Random Forest for variance reduction.
    Final prediction = 0.6 * GB + 0.4 * RF.
  - Dropped noisy composition features (20 dims averaged over full sequence);
    kept only high-signal differential and positional features.
  - Added more physicochemical scales (flexibility, secondary structure
    propensity, isoelectric-point proxy, accessible surface area proxy).
  - Added pairwise interaction terms between wt/mut properties.
  - Tm_wt (wild-type melting temp) is the single strongest predictor; kept.
  - Hyperparameters tuned to dataset size (~1000-2000 training rows).
"""

import os
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingRegressor, RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# ── paths ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA_DIR = "benchmark/datasets/pucci"
PDB_DIR  = os.path.join(DATA_DIR, "PDBs")
OUT_DIR  = "pred_results"
OUT_FILE = os.path.join(OUT_DIR, "pucci-proteins_test_pred.csv")

# ── amino acid dictionaries ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THREE_TO_ONE = {
    "ALA": "A", "ARG": "R", "ASN": "N", "ASP": "D", "CYS": "C",
    "GLN": "Q", "GLU": "E", "GLY": "G", "HIS": "H", "ILE": "I",
    "LEU": "L", "LYS": "K", "MET": "M", "PHE": "F", "PRO": "P",
    "SER": "S", "THR": "T", "TRP": "W", "TYR": "Y", "VAL": "V",
    "MSE": "M",
}

AA_ORDER = list("ACDEFGHIKLMNPQRSTVWY")
AA_INDEX = {aa: i for i, aa in enumerate(AA_ORDER)}

# Kyte-Doolittle hydrophobicity
HYDROPHOBICITY = {
    "A":  1.8, "R": -4.5, "N": -3.5, "D": -3.5, "C":  2.5,
    "Q": -3.5, "E": -3.5, "G": -0.4, "H": -3.2, "I":  4.5,
    "L":  3.8, "K": -3.9, "M":  1.9, "F":  2.8, "P": -1.6,
    "S": -0.8, "T": -0.7, "W": -0.9, "Y": -1.3, "V":  4.2,
}

# Formal charge at pH 7
CHARGE = {
    "A":  0, "R": +1, "N":  0, "D": -1, "C":  0,
    "Q":  0, "E": -1, "G":  0, "H":  0, "I":  0,
    "L":  0, "K": +1, "M":  0, "F":  0, "P":  0,
    "S":  0, "T":  0, "W":  0, "Y":  0, "V":  0,
}

# Molecular weight (approximate, Da)
MOL_WEIGHT = {
    "A":  89, "R": 174, "N": 132, "D": 133, "C": 121,
    "Q": 146, "E": 147, "G":  75, "H": 155, "I": 131,
    "L": 131, "K": 146, "M": 149, "F": 165, "P": 115,
    "S": 105, "T": 119, "W": 204, "Y": 181, "V": 117,
}

# van der Waals volume (Å³)
VDW_VOLUME = {
    "A":  67, "R": 148, "N": 114, "D": 111, "C":  86,
    "Q": 128, "E": 138, "G":  48, "H": 118, "I": 124,
    "L": 124, "K": 135, "M": 124, "F": 135, "P":  90,
    "S":  73, "T":  93, "W": 163, "Y": 141, "V": 105,
}

# Polarity (Zimmermann)
POLARITY = {
    "A":  0.0,  "R": 52.0, "N":  3.38, "D": 49.7, "C":  1.48,
    "Q":  3.53, "E": 49.9, "G":   0.0, "H": 51.6, "I":  0.13,
    "L":  0.13, "K": 49.5, "M":  1.43, "F":  0.35, "P":  1.58,
    "S":  1.67, "T":  1.66, "W":  2.10, "Y":  1.61, "V":  0.13,
}

# B-factor / flexibility (Vihinen et al.)
FLEXIBILITY = {
    "A": 0.360, "R": 0.530, "N": 0.460, "D": 0.510, "C": 0.350,
    "Q": 0.490, "E": 0.500, "G": 0.540, "H": 0.320, "I": 0.460,
    "L": 0.370, "K": 0.470, "M": 0.300, "F": 0.310, "P": 0.510,
    "S": 0.510, "T": 0.440, "W": 0.310, "Y": 0.420, "V": 0.390,
}

# Alpha-helix propensity (Chou & Fasman)
HELIX_PROP = {
    "A": 1.45, "R": 0.98, "N": 0.73, "D": 0.98, "C": 0.77,
    "Q": 1.17, "E": 1.53, "G": 0.53, "H": 1.24, "I": 1.00,
    "L": 1.34, "K": 1.07, "M": 1.20, "F": 1.12, "P": 0.59,
    "S": 0.79, "T": 0.82, "W": 1.14, "Y": 0.61, "V": 1.14,
}

# Beta-sheet propensity (Chou & Fasman)
SHEET_PROP = {
    "A": 0.97, "R": 0.88, "N": 0.65, "D": 0.80, "C": 1.30,
    "Q": 1.23, "E": 0.26, "G": 0.81, "H": 0.71, "I": 1.60,
    "L": 1.22, "K": 0.74, "M": 1.67, "F": 1.28, "P": 0.62,
    "S": 0.72, "T": 1.20, "W": 1.19, "Y": 1.29, "V": 1.65,
}

# Accessible surface area (Ala=1 normalised, Miller et al.)
ASA = {
    "A": 1.00, "R": 0.84, "N": 0.63, "D": 0.89, "C": 0.36,
    "Q": 0.73, "E": 0.83, "G": 0.71, "H": 0.64, "I": 0.60,
    "L": 0.73, "K": 0.93, "M": 0.70, "F": 0.62, "P": 0.76,
    "S": 0.78, "T": 0.70, "W": 0.55, "Y": 0.63, "V": 0.64,
}


def aa_props(aa: str) -> np.ndarray:
    """Return 9-dim physicochemical feature vector for a single amino acid."""
    return np.array([
        HYDROPHOBICITY.get(aa, 0.0),
        CHARGE.get(aa, 0.0),
        MOL_WEIGHT.get(aa, 120.0),
        VDW_VOLUME.get(aa, 100.0),
        POLARITY.get(aa, 0.0),
        FLEXIBILITY.get(aa, 0.40),
        HELIX_PROP.get(aa, 1.0),
        SHEET_PROP.get(aa, 1.0),
        ASA.get(aa, 0.70),
    ], dtype=np.float64)


def parse_chain_a_sequence(pdb_id: str) -> dict:
    """Return {res_number: one_letter_code} for chain A of the given PDB."""
    path = os.path.join(PDB_DIR, f"{pdb_id.lower()}.pdb")
    seq = {}
    seen = set()
    try:
        with open(path) as fh:
            for line in fh:
                if not line.startswith("ATOM"):
                    continue
                if line[21] != "A":
                    continue
                res3 = line[17:20].strip()
                try:
                    resn = int(line[22:26].strip())
                except ValueError:
                    continue
                aa1 = THREE_TO_ONE.get(res3)
                if aa1 and resn not in seen:
                    seen.add(resn)
                    seq[resn] = aa1
    except FileNotFoundError:
        pass
    return seq


def make_mutant_sequence(wt_seq: dict, resn: int, mut_aa1: str) -> list:
    seq = dict(wt_seq)
    if resn in seq:
        seq[resn] = mut_aa1
    return [seq[k] for k in sorted(seq.keys())]


def wt_composition_features(wt_seq_dict: dict) -> np.ndarray:
    """20-dim amino acid composition of the WT sequence (position-independent)."""
    arr = np.zeros(20, dtype=np.float64)
    seq_list = list(wt_seq_dict.values())
    if not seq_list:
        return arr
    for aa in seq_list:
        if aa in AA_INDEX:
            arr[AA_INDEX[aa]] += 1
    arr /= len(seq_list)
    return arr


def local_context_features(wt_seq: dict, resn: int, window: int = 4) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Average physicochemical properties of the ±window residues around
    the mutation site (9 dims).  Captures local structural environment.
    """
    sorted_resns = sorted(wt_seq.keys())
    if not sorted_resns:
        return np.zeros(9, dtype=np.float64)

    # Find index of mutation residue in the sorted list
    try:
        idx = sorted_resns.index(resn)
    except ValueError:
        # resn not in wt_seq; use middle of sequence
        idx = len(sorted_resns) // 2

    lo = max(0, idx - window)
    hi = min(len(sorted_resns), idx + window + 1)
    neighbours = [wt_seq[sorted_resns[i]] for i in range(lo, hi)
                  if sorted_resns[i] != resn]  # exclude mutation site itself

    if not neighbours:
        return np.zeros(9, dtype=np.float64)
    return np.mean([aa_props(a) for a in neighbours], axis=0)


def build_features(df: pd.DataFrame, wt_seqs: dict) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Feature vector per row (total = 20+9+9+9+9+3+1+1+1 = 62 dims):
      20 : WT amino acid composition of full sequence
       9 : WT residue physicochemical properties
       9 : Mutant residue physicochemical properties
       9 : Difference (mut - wt) properties  <-- highest signal
       9 : Local context (avg of ±4 neighbours)
       1 : Tmexp [wt]
       1 : sequence length
       1 : relative position (resn / seq_len)
       1 : is_charged_change (1 if charge sign differs else 0)
       1 : is_pro_gly_change (to/from Pro or Gly)
       1 : vol_ratio (mut_vol / wt_vol)
    Total: 62 dims
    """
    rows = []
    for _, row in df.iterrows():
        pdb_id  = str(row["PDBid"]).lower()
        resn    = int(row["RESN"])
        res_wt  = str(row["RESwt"]).strip()
        res_mut = str(row["RESmut"]).strip()

        try:
            tm_wt = float(row["Tmexp [wt]"])
        except (ValueError, KeyError):
            tm_wt = 50.0

        wt_aa1  = THREE_TO_ONE.get(res_wt,  "G")
        mut_aa1 = THREE_TO_ONE.get(res_mut, "G")

        wt_seq = wt_seqs.get(pdb_id, {})
        seq_len = len(wt_seq) if wt_seq else 100
        rel_pos = resn / seq_len if seq_len > 0 else 0.5

        # Compositional features (WT whole-sequence)
        comp = wt_composition_features(wt_seq)

        # Residue-level features
        props_wt   = aa_props(wt_aa1)
        props_mut  = aa_props(mut_aa1)
        props_diff = props_mut - props_wt

        # Local context
        local_ctx = local_context_features(wt_seq, resn, window=4)

        # Extra binary / ratio features
        is_charged_change = float(
            int(CHARGE.get(wt_aa1, 0) != 0) != int(CHARGE.get(mut_aa1, 0) != 0)
        )
        is_pro_gly = float(wt_aa1 in "PG" or mut_aa1 in "PG")
        wt_vol  = VDW_VOLUME.get(wt_aa1,  100.0)
        mut_vol = VDW_VOLUME.get(mut_aa1, 100.0)
        vol_ratio = mut_vol / wt_vol if wt_vol > 0 else 1.0

        scalar_feats = np.array(
            [tm_wt, float(seq_len), rel_pos, is_charged_change, is_pro_gly, vol_ratio],
            dtype=np.float64
        )

        feat = np.concatenate([comp, props_wt, props_mut, props_diff,
                               local_ctx, scalar_feats])
        rows.append(feat)

    return np.array(rows, dtype=np.float64)


def find_target_column(df: pd.DataFrame) -> str:
    """Robustly locate the deltaTm column."""
    if "ΔTmexp" in df.columns:
        return "ΔTmexp"
    for col in df.columns:
        cl = col.lower()
        if "tm" in cl and "exp" in cl and "wt" not in cl:
            return col
    if len(df.columns) > 6:
        return df.columns[6]
    raise ValueError(f"Cannot find deltaTm column. Columns: {list(df.columns)}")


def main():
    os.makedirs(OUT_DIR, exist_ok=True)

    # Load data
    train_df = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA_DIR, "pucci-proteins_train.csv"), index_col=0)
    test_df  = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA_DIR, "pucci-proteins_test.csv"),  index_col=0)
    dev_df   = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA_DIR, "pucci-proteins_dev.csv"),   index_col=0)

    # Combine train + dev
    fit_df = pd.concat([train_df, dev_df], ignore_index=True)

    target_col = find_target_column(fit_df)
    print(f"Target column: {repr(target_col)}")

    # Pre-parse all PDB sequences once
    all_pdb_ids = (
        set(fit_df["PDBid"].str.lower().unique()) |
        set(test_df["PDBid"].str.lower().unique())
    )
    wt_seqs = {pid: parse_chain_a_sequence(pid) for pid in all_pdb_ids}

    # Build feature matrices
    X_train = build_features(fit_df,  wt_seqs)
    X_test  = build_features(test_df, wt_seqs)
    y_train = fit_df[target_col].astype(float).values

    # Scale (GBR is tree-based and doesn't require it, but helps RF)
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    X_train_s = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
    X_test_s  = scaler.transform(X_test)

    # ── Model 1: Gradient Boosting Regressor ─────────────────────────────────
    gb = GradientBoostingRegressor(
        n_estimators=500,
        learning_rate=0.05,
        max_depth=4,
        min_samples_leaf=5,
        subsample=0.8,
        max_features="sqrt",
        random_state=42,
    )
    gb.fit(X_train_s, y_train)

    # ── Model 2: Random Forest Regressor ─────────────────────────────────────
    rf = RandomForestRegressor(
        n_estimators=500,
        max_depth=None,
        min_samples_leaf=3,
        max_features="sqrt",
        random_state=42,
        n_jobs=-1,
    )
    rf.fit(X_train_s, y_train)

    # ── Ensemble: weighted average ────────────────────────────────────────────
    preds_gb = gb.predict(X_test_s)
    preds_rf = rf.predict(X_test_s)
    preds = 0.6 * preds_gb + 0.4 * preds_rf

    # Save
    out_df = pd.DataFrame({"deltaTm": preds})
    out_df.to_csv(OUT_FILE, index=False)
    print(f"Saved {len(preds)} predictions to {OUT_FILE}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

r3_reasoning.content:
# Round 3 Reasoning

## Approach

**Diagnosis of round 2 (MAE 64.3):** The MLP-based script ran without errors and produced 147 predictions, but the MAE of ~64 °C is dramatically worse than a naïve mean predictor would achieve (typical deltaTm range is roughly ±20 °C). This indicates the MLP was not learning meaningful signal — likely due to a combination of (a) too many noisy composition features drowning out the real signal, and (b) MLPs requiring more data than this dataset provides to converge reliably.

**Round 3 strategy:**
1. Replace the MLP with a gradient-boosted ensemble (GBR + RF) — tree-based models are far more data-efficient on small tabular datasets (~1000–2000 rows) and are less sensitive to irrelevant features.
2. Slim down features: drop the 20-dim mutant composition vector (noisy, captures little about the mutation effect) and replace the 20-dim WT composition. The real signal lives in the **differential physicochemical properties** (9 dims, mut − wt) and the **Tmexp [wt]** scalar. All other features are secondary.
3. Enrich the physicochemical representation from 5 to 9 properties per residue by adding flexibility (B-factor proxy), helix/sheet propensity (Chou-Fasman), and accessible surface area — all biologically relevant to thermal stability.
4. Add a **local context feature** (average properties of ±4 neighbouring residues around the mutation site), capturing the structural environment.
5. Add simple binary/ratio flags: charge-sign change, proline/glycine involvement, and volume ratio — known to have outsized stability effects.
6. Ensemble: GBR (0.6 weight) + RF (0.4 weight). GBR is typically stronger on tabular regression; RF adds variance reduction.

## Library choices

- `sklearn.ensemble.GradientBoostingRegressor` / `RandomForestRegressor`: robust, well-calibrated, no GPU needed, directly available in the environment.
- `sklearn.preprocessing.StandardScaler`: still applied so RF benefits from scaled input.
- `pandas`, `numpy`: data loading and feature construction.
- No PDB-parsing library — custom ATOM-record parser avoids a heavy dependency.

## Risks

- The dataset is small; GBR can overfit if `n_estimators` is too large. Mitigated by `learning_rate=0.05`, `subsample=0.8`, and `min_samples_leaf=5`.
- Some PDB files may have insertion codes or non-standard residue numbering. The parser silently skips these; the mutation at the listed `RESN` may not be found in `wt_seq`, in which case the local-context and composition features degrade to zeros — same fallback as round 2.
- If the true deltaTm signal is primarily driven by factors not encoded in sequence (e.g., solvent conditions, oligomeric state), no sequence-based model will achieve low MAE regardless of architecture.

## Use of memory

Round 1 established that the target column detection and PDB parsing were sound. Round 2 confirmed the data pipeline works end-to-end (exit 0, correct file written) but that the MLP architecture was insufficient — MAE barely changed from round 1 (~67 → 64). This pointed squarely to the model family rather than data issues. Round 3 therefore focuses entirely on switching to a tree-based ensemble and engineering more discriminative features, leaving the validated data pipeline largely intact.

rounds_summary.attempted_route: sklearn MLPRegressor (256,128,64), 63-dim features: 20-dim AA composition + Tmwt scalar + sequence length + wt/mut one-hot encodings. StandardScaler, Adam, early stopping.
rounds_summary.observation: Exit 0, 147 rows saved. MAE=67.15°C — far above naive baseline for a target with typical range ±20°C, indicating near-zero predictive signal.

rounds_summary.attempted_route: Deeper MLPRegressor (512,256,128,64), 78-dim features: added 5 physicochemical props per residue (hydrophobicity, charge, MW, vdW volume, polarity), their mut-wt diffs, and relative mutation position.
rounds_summary.observation: Exit 0, 147 rows saved. MAE=64.32°C — marginal 4% improvement despite substantially richer features; MLP still predicts near-constant output.

rounds_summary.attempted_route: GradientBoostingRegressor+RandomForest ensemble (0.6/0.4 weighted), 62-dim features: expanded to 9 physicochemical props (added flexibility, helix/sheet propensity, ASA), local ±4 residue context, binary charge-change and Pro/Gly flags.
rounds_summary.observation: Exit 0, 147 rows saved. MAE=66.27°C — switching to tree ensembles and adding structural propensity features gave no improvement; MAE regressed slightly vs round 2.

ruled_out_routes: sklearn MLPRegressor with hidden layers (256,128,64) or (512,256,128,64) + StandardScaler + Adam + early stopping on hand-crafted tabular features (MAE ~67°C).
sklearn GradientBoostingRegressor (n_estimators=500, lr=0.05, max_depth=4) + RandomForest ensemble on physicochemical differential features (MAE ~66°C).
20-dim amino acid composition of full mutant/WT sequence as primary feature block — demonstrated noisy and non-informative for deltaTm prediction across two rounds.
Hand-crafted local context window (±4 residues, averaged 9 physicochemical props) as structural environment proxy — did not recover meaningful signal in round 3.
content.synthesised_diagnosis: Across all three rounds the agent kept running into a representation ceiling: every approach relied exclusively on per-residue physicochemical lookup tables and sequence composition, which encode only amino acid identity, not 3D structural context. The mutation site's burial depth, hydrogen-bond network, and backbone strain — the primary determinants of deltaTm — are invisible to these features. No model family (MLP, GBR, RF) or feature enrichment within this hand-crafted tabular paradigm can recover that information, so MAE is locked near ~65°C regardless of architecture.
content.rationale: All three model families converged to MAE ~65°C with no improvement trajectory, confirming the failure is not architectural but representational: hand-crafted physicochemical features cannot encode the 3D structural determinants of thermal stability, making further tuning within this paradigm futile.
content.recommended_alternative: Use the ESM-2 protein language model (Meta, available via `fair-esm` pip package) to extract per-residue embeddings for both WT and mutant sequences. Compute the difference embedding at the mutation position (esm2_mut[resn] - esm2_wt[resn], 1280-dim for esm2_t33_650M_UR50D), concatenate with Tmexp_wt scalar, then fit a Ridge or LightGBM regressor. ESM-2 encodes learned structural context from 250M proteins and has been shown to correlate with stability in prior benchmarks.
